text.title: Design Review PR Checklist Core Rules
text.summary: A PR-time design-QA checklist (10 items across token usage, component reuse, motion compliance, accessibility, content rules, breakpoint coverage) that gates whether the implementation matches the design handoff.

rule.statement: Every UI-touching PR MUST be checked against all 10 items: tokens, components, states (default/hover/focus/active/disabled/loading/error/empty), motion (prefers-reduced-motion), a11y (focus order, ARIA, contrast), content rules, breakpoints, image handling, dark-mode parity, cross-browser smoke.

rationale.source: Material Design QA Checklist; Polaris adoption metrics

rule.rationale: 10 items map empirically to post-release defect modes; the set fits a 15-min PR review.

rule.statement: A named designer or design-engineer MUST be a required PR reviewer for UI-touching PRs. Generic dev peer review does not satisfy design adherence.

rationale.source: Atlassian Design QA practice; Polaris case studies

rule.rationale: PRs without designer review show 2-3x higher post-release design-bug rate.

rule.statement: Each item check MUST include evidence: PR diff link, screenshot/recording, or script output. Pass-without-evidence is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Skim-review at PR time has same defect-find rate as no-review on design items; evidence forces actual look.

rule.statement: The checklist STAYS in its lane: design-system adherence, accessibility, content rules. It does NOT cover generic code style, test coverage, or business-logic correctness.
rule.rationale: Reviews trying to cover everything cover nothing well.

rule.statement: Implementation deviation from the handoff MUST be flagged back to the designer for accept-or-rework; silent acceptance is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Silent deviations decay the design system; explicit feedback either updates the system or reverts the code.

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — no design system; single-person dev-and-designer team; prototype/spike PR marked experimental; project phase explicitly out-of-scope), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
